retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Temporomandibular Joint -- Structure and Function -- Articular Disc
retrieved_docs.content: The anterior portion of the articular disc contacts the joint capsule, articular eminence, condyle, and the upper area of the lateral pterygoid muscle. The posterior segment of the articular disc relates to the bilateral retrodiscal tissue located behind the condyle, the glenoid fossa, the condyle itself, and the temporal bone. The medial and lateral aspects of the cartilaginous disc attach to the mandibular condyle. The disc’s edges partially fuse with the fibrous capsule surrounding the joint.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Temporomandibular Joint -- Structure and Function -- Articular Disc
retrieved_docs.content: The articular disc covering the condyle and interposed beneath the glenoid fossa has a biconcave or oval shape. The cartilaginous disc features an anterior portion measuring about 2 mm and a posterior portion approximately 3 mm thick, with a thinner middle region. The anterior portion consists of an upper fibroelastic fascia layer and a lower fibrous layer. The upper part contacts the postglenoid process, preventing the disc from slipping during mouth opening. The lower part restricts excessive rotational movement of the disc relative to the mandibular condyle.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Temporomandibular Joint -- Clinical Significance -- Instrumental Assessments
retrieved_docs.content: Instrumental assessments are essential for accurate diagnosis and should be performed before initiating any therapy for TMJ dysfunction. [19] Panoramic radiography allows evaluation of the relationship between the mandibular condyles and the corresponding glenoid fossae. Multislice or cone-beam computed tomography enables detailed assessment of the morphology of TMJ components, bone position, and potential pathologies. Magnetic resonance imaging provides visualization of the articular disc, as well as joint anatomy, function, and form.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Occlusal Contact Relations And Mandibular Movements -- Structure and Function -- Temporomandibular Joint (TMJ)
retrieved_docs.content: Condylar movements during mastication are complex.  In total, condylar actions within the temporomandibular joints have 3 basic movements: spin, roll, and slide. Spin describes the condylar head twisting about a vertical axis within the capsule. Roll is the condylar head rocking back and forth with the central portion maintaining contact with the intra-capsular disc. Slide refers to the translation of the condylar head relative to the intra-capsular disc.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Temporomandibular Joint -- Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: The relationship between TMJ function, anatomy, and posture remains a topic of considerable debate. Difficulty in establishing a definitive link arises from the anatomical complexity of the TMJ, which includes the capsule, muscles, ligaments, nerves, and articular disc, and from challenges in comparing its function with other regions of the body. Research has not yet identified a standardized unit of measurement or an ideal tool to assess the relationship between TMJ and posture. Consideration of the patient’s clinical history and the duration of joint dysfunction is essential for proper evaluation.

retrieved_docs.title: Temporomandibular Syndrome -- Pathophysiology -- Deviation in the Form
retrieved_docs.content: The physiological aging or minor degeneration of the condyle, disc, and fossa can cause deviations and dysfunction, significantly affecting mandibular movements.

retrieved_docs.title: Temporomandibular Syndrome -- Pathophysiology -- Derangement of the Condyle-Disc Complex
retrieved_docs.content: The derangement of the condyle disc complex arises due to a breakdown in the rotational function of the disc. This condition can result from the lengthening of ligaments (discal collateral and inferior retro-discal ligaments) or thinning of the posterior disc border. The contributing factors can be micro or macro trauma.

retrieved_docs.title: Temporomandibular Syndrome -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is the preferred imaging study to assess the TMJ due to its capacity to accurately show joint effusion, disc displacement, and soft tissues. [15] However, a simple panoramic X-ray can help rapidly evaluate the state of the dentition and joint and tends to be the initial investigation. Plain radiographs and CT scans can show severe joint degeneration, fractures, and dislocations. [1] Ultrasonography shows the disc position, but it does not help diagnose osteoarthritis. [1] Computed tomography (CT) and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) are more beneficial in severe, chronic, or suspected structural abnormalities of TMJ. [16] [17] Additionally, newer techniques of nerve blocks, botulinum toxin injections, arthrography, and mandibular motion data can prove to be of significant diagnostic benefit.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Temporomandibular Joint -- Structure and Function -- Bony Components of the TMJ
retrieved_docs.content: The anterior boundary of the glenoid fossa is the articular eminence, a medial bony prominence at the posterior border of the zygomatic bone. The preglenoid plane, which is slightly inclined, leads into the articular eminence, which lies anterior to the fossa along the base of the skull. This region facilitates the movements of the articular disc and condyle. On the lateral surface of the articular eminence, near the root of the zygomatic process, lies a bony ridge known as the articular tubercle.

retrieved_docs.title: Temporomandibular Syndrome -- Introduction -- Temporomandibular Joint Anatomy
retrieved_docs.content: The temporomandibular joint (TMJ) is a ginglymoarthrodial joint formed by the glenoid fossa of the temporal bone and the mandibular condyle. [1] An articular disc separates the joint into two synovial cavities with distinctive movement patterns. Gliding or translatory movement occurs in the superior joint (between the articular disc and the glenoid fossa). [2] Rotary or hinge movement takes place in the inferior joint (between the articular disc and the condyle). [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Temporomandibular Joint -- Structure and Function -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: During mouth opening, the TMJ exhibits a combination of rotational movement within the discomandibular space and translational movement within the discotemporal space, with rotation occurring before translation. The condyle can move laterally through rotation, followed by anterior sliding of the same condylar structure, while the contralateral condyle undergoes anterior translation and rotation medially. Posterior movement of one condyle occurs simultaneously with anterior sliding of the opposite condyle. Bilateral or ipsilateral TMJ protrusion results from anterior sliding of the condyles.

retrieved_docs.title: Temporomandibular Syndrome -- Pathophysiology -- Adherences and Adhesions
retrieved_docs.content: An adherence represents a brief hold of the articular surfaces. Adhesion can happen between the condyle and disc or the disc and fossa. Adhesions result from a fibrous connective tissue or loss of lubrication between the structures. It is characterized by restriction in the normal translation of the condyle movement with no pain. In chronic situations, the patient senses an inability to get the teeth back to occlusion during the closure.

retrieved_docs.title: Temporomandibular Syndrome -- Pathophysiology -- Osteoarthrosis
retrieved_docs.content: Arthrosis is the adaptive unaltered arthritic changes of the bone due to decreased bone loading. It occurs after the overloading of the joint, mainly due to parafunctional activity, and is often associated with disc dislocation.

retrieved_docs.title: Temporomandibular Syndrome -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Intraarticular disorders refer to inflammatory or mechanical factors that affect the joint itself, articular disc displacement being the most common. [1] Other intraarticular causes include trauma, capsular inflammation, osteoarthritis, hypermobility, and inflammatory diseases, like rheumatoid arthritis. It is not clear yet if malocclusion contributes to TMD. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Occlusal Contact Relations And Mandibular Movements -- Structure and Function -- Temporomandibular Joint (TMJ)
retrieved_docs.content: The temporomandibular joint is the junction of the mandible and the temporal bone. These are synovial joints, each with an upper and lower compartment divided by an intra-capsular disc. The mandibular condyle articulates with the intra-capsular disc and remains in the mandibular fossa at rest when the jaw is closed. The condyle follows the articular eminence forward during jaw opening.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Temporomandibular Joint -- Clinical Significance -- Clinical Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Clinical evaluation of the TMJ begins with observation of natural mandibular movements. Assessment includes facial symmetry, the range and quality of TMJ movements, the onset and severity of pain during opening, closing, and accessory movements, and the presence of joint sounds.

retrieved_docs.content: pathologies, entrapment syndromes, headache and facial‑nerve disorders, musculoskeletal pathology and diagnostic imaging, thereby supporting comprehensive patient assessment, precise diagnosis and targeted clinical management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1866
retrieved_docs.content: The term “Division of joint capsule, ligament, or cartilage” functions as a classification category that organizes the anatomical subdivisions referred to as “division of joint capsule; ligament or cartilage,” thereby grouping these related joint structures under a common descriptive heading.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 143
retrieved_docs.content: Arthropathy associated with an endocrine AND/OR metabolic disorder is classified as other non‑traumatic joint disorders and its primary finding site is the joints (klouby). Other non‑traumatic joint disorders are linked to the concept of اعتلال مفصلي (joint disease). In the context of surgical procedures, Division of joint capsule, ligament, or cartilage is a subcategory (has parent) of Incision and excision of joint structures and it classifies the anatomical category “division of joint capsule; ligament or cartilage.” Incision and excision of joint structures also has a relationship with the joints (klouby). This integrated knowledge connects endocrine‑related arthropathy to non‑traumatic joint pathology, emphasizes its joint involvement, and outlines the hierarchical relationship between joint‑focused surgical interventions and the specific joint structures they address.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 2605
retrieved_docs.content: Migraine and Headache Syndromes are classified under the broader category of اعتلال عصبي عضلي (neuromuscular disorder). Neuromuscular disorder is associated with شلل (paralysis), indicating that dysfunction of nerves and muscles can lead to loss of motor function. Other disorders of synovium, tendon, and bursa are linked to اضطراب العضلات (muscle disorder), which in turn is also related to شلل, highlighting that pathological changes in musculoskeletal structures may contribute to paralysis. Specific nerve pathologies such as آفة في العصب الوركي (sciatic nerve lesion) and اضطراب العصب الوجهي (facial nerve disorder) have a narrower relationship with شلل, underscoring that localized nerve injuries can directly result in paralysis. Collectively, these relationships illustrate how various headache syndromes, musculoskeletal disorders, and peripheral nerve lesions are interconnected through neuromuscular and muscle dysfunctions that may culminate in paralysis, emphasizing the importance of comprehensive assessment and management of these conditions in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 2 -- Community 984
retrieved_docs.content: Neuromuscular disease (اعتلال عصبي عضلي) is connected to paralysis (شلل), indicating that disorders affecting both nerves and muscles can result in loss of motor function. Muscle disorder (اضطراب العضلات) also has a relationship with paralysis, reinforcing the link between impaired muscle function and the development of paralysis. Other disorders of synovium, tendon, and bursa are related to muscle disorder, suggesting that pathological changes in joint‑related structures can influence muscle health and thereby contribute to paralytic outcomes. More specific conditions, such as lesion in the sciatic nerve (آفة في العصب الوركي) and facial nerve disorder (اضطراب العصب الوجهي), are identified as narrower relationships to paralysis, highlighting that focal nerve injuries or dysfunctions are precise causes of paralytic deficits. Together, these concepts illustrate how a spectrum of neuromuscular, muscular, and peripheral nerve pathologies are interrelated and can culminate in paralysis, underscoring the importance of evaluating both muscle and nerve integrity in patients presenting with motor impairment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1853
retrieved_docs.content: Other OR therapeutic procedures on joints is a classification that categorizes other or therapeutic procedures on joints.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2851
retrieved_docs.content: Other non‑traumatic joint disorders are closely associated with joint disease (اعتلال مفصلي), indicating that a range of non‑injury‑related joint conditions fall under the broader category of joint disease. This relationship highlights the importance of recognizing various non‑traumatic joint pathologies as part of the overall spectrum of اعتلال مفصلي for accurate diagnosis and comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 269
retrieved_docs.content: concepts (pain, paralysis) with anatomical sites (peripheral nerves, specific cranial and spinal nerves, tendon sheaths, synovium), disease entities (headache, ocular disorders, the extensive list of neuropathies, muscular disorders, entrapment syndromes, rare hereditary and inflammatory conditions), and procedural interventions (nerve incision, decompression), providing clinicians with a comprehensive taxonomy for precise diagnosis, coding, and patient‑centered management of nervous‑system and sense‑organ disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2234
retrieved_docs.content: Other disorders of synovium, tendon, and bursa—conditions affecting the connective tissues that line joints, support tendons, and cushion bursae—are related to muscle disorders (اضطراب العضلات), highlighting an interconnection between joint‑associated soft‑tissue pathologies and muscular dysfunction.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 256
retrieved_docs.content: diverse injury types (intracranial, skeletal, facial), their specific anatomical subcategories, corresponding treatment modalities, and long‑term sequelae are interrelated, while also incorporating psychosocial determinants such as aggression that may influence injury occurrence and outcome. This integrated framework supports comprehensive assessment, classification, and management of patients presenting with complex traumatic or toxic presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 2361
retrieved_docs.content: Headache; including migraine is classified as a condition that belongs to the broader category of Nervous system and sense organ diseases. The category Nervous system and sense organ diseases also includes the child entity اضطراب العين (eye disorder). Recognizing that both headache—including migraine—and eye disorders are subtypes of Nervous system and sense organ diseases helps clinicians organize related symptoms, consider shared pathophysiological mechanisms, and guide appropriate diagnostic and therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2159
retrieved_docs.content: Migraine and Headache Syndromes are categorized as a subcategory of the broader condition known as اعتلال عصبي عضلي (neuromuscular disease). This hierarchical relationship suggests that migraine and headache disorders may be viewed within the context of neuromuscular pathology, highlighting the importance of considering underlying neuromuscular factors when diagnosing and managing patients presenting with these headache syndromes.

This integrated framework aids clinicians in selecting appropriate specialties, understanding procedural relationships, and recognizing how metabolic or endocrine disorders contribute to non‑traumatic joint disease.

retrieved_docs.content: Other OR therapeutic procedures on musculoskeletal system is a classification term that categorizes other or therapeutic procedures on the musculoskeletal system, grouping together various interventions aimed at treating musculoskeletal conditions.
